title: Hyderabad to Leh: 2,800 Kilometers and Everything In Between
description: The full journey from the Deccan plateau to the roof of India — what it takes to plan it, survive it, and understand why you needed it.

sections.heading: Why the Full Distance
paragraphs: Flying to Manali and riding from there is the common approach. It saves five days and makes logistical sense. It is also not what I needed.

I needed the landscape to change slowly under my wheels — the Deccan plateau giving way to Rajasthan's scrubland, Rajasthan sharpening into the Himalayan foothills, and finally the foothills crumbling into Ladakh's moonscape. Each transition takes days. Each one earns the next. You cannot appreciate Leh if you land in Manali. You can only arrive there.
2,800 kilometers. Fifteen days. Solo.

sections.heading: Day 1–2: Hyderabad to Nagpur
paragraphs: Day 1 is a long flat highway run to get the kilometers behind you. NH44 most of the way. Leave at 4 AM, arrive in Nagpur by evening. Nothing glamorous. Everything necessary.
The Deccan plateau in the early morning is still and orange. Trucks thin out after Nalgonda. By Adilabad the road is almost empty and you settle into the pace of the day.

sections.heading: Day 3–5: Nagpur to Delhi Through Bundelkhand
paragraphs: Central India at its most honest. Flat, wide, sun-baked. Stop at Orchha — a fortified town that most people drive past without seeing, which is their loss. The cenotaphs on the river banks are spectacular and completely unvisited at 7 AM.
Agra before Delhi. The Taj at sunrise is worth the 4:30 AM alarm. By 9 AM it is a crowd scene. At 6 AM it is still. Delhi is just the overnight stop before the real ride begins.

sections.heading: Day 6–7: Delhi to Manali
paragraphs: The traffic through Chandigarh thins out past Bilaspur. Then the road starts climbing and the Kullu valley opens up — green and narrow and spectacular even in the haze.
Manali is where you service the bike, eat well, sleep properly, and prepare. Two nights minimum. This is also where altitude acclimatization begins — stay hydrated, eat light, sleep at 11 PM.

sections.heading: Day 8: Manali to Keylong — The Rohtang Crossing
paragraphs: The road out of Manali is deceiving — smooth tarmac through pine forest, then the Rohtang tunnel opens up and the landscape changes completely. Brown. Vast. The kind of emptiness that takes time to read.
The Himalayan handled Rohtang without complaint. 70 km/h, steady throttle, no drama. Keylong by evening, a guesthouse with butter tea and zero questions.

sections.heading: Day 9: Keylong to Sarchu — Baralacha La at 4,890m
paragraphs: The road is gravel and rock from Zing Zing Bar onwards — the kind of surface that rewards slow, deliberate riding. The summit is quiet in a way that cities never are. Not silent — the wind is constant — but quiet in that it demands nothing from you.
Sarchu is a tent city at 4,200 meters. Dal rice and ten hours of sleep.

sections.heading: Day 10: Sarchu to Leh — Three More Passes
paragraphs: Lachulung La, Nakee La, Tanglang La — three passes in a single day. The road is better here, the views are absurd in the best possible way, and somewhere past Pang the Indus valley opens up below you and you think: I rode here.
Leh by 4 PM. Parked outside a café near the main market. Ordered coffee. Sat for forty-five minutes without looking at my phone. That was the arrival.

sections.heading: Leh: Four Days at the Roof
paragraphs: One day of complete rest. One day at Pangong Lake — the real one, not the film set version. One day at Nubra Valley across Khardung La (5,359 meters, one of the highest motorable roads on earth). And one day just sitting in the main market drinking butter tea.
The fourth day was the best one. The mountains don't move. The tea gets refilled. That is enough.
Bike trailered back to Delhi. Flew home. Some things you ride once in each direction. Some things you just do once and carry the weight of it instead.

title: Hampi: Riding Into the Ruins of an Empire
description: 370 kilometers from Hyderabad to the ancient capital of Vijayanagara — boulders, temples, and a landscape so strange it takes a day to stop questioning it.

sections.heading: The Ride There
paragraphs: Hyderabad to Hampi is 370 kilometers on NH44 through Kurnool and into Karnataka. Leave before 6 AM — the traffic is manageable early, unreasonable by 8.

The last 50 kilometers are the most interesting. The boulder fields appear gradually at first, scattered across the hilltops. Then, all at once, you are surrounded — granite formations of improbable shapes, orange in the afternoon light, stretching as far as you can see.
Arrive in the late afternoon. The evening light on the ruins is something different from midday.

sections.heading: Virupaksha Temple
paragraphs: Still active, over 1,000 years old, anchoring the main bazaar. The elephant Lakshmi who lives there will bless you for a banana. She is worth the interaction.
Come at 6 AM before the day starts. The gopuram in the early light looks like it was built yesterday and also like it has always been here.

sections.heading: Vittala Temple and the Stone Chariot
paragraphs: The famous stone chariot and the musical pillars. Come early — by mid-morning the tourist groups arrive and the stillness disappears. In the first hour after opening, you can stand at the chariot alone and hear the Tungabhadra a kilometer away.

The pillars actually produce tonal sounds when struck — different columns, different notes. The Archaeological Survey has understandably asked people to stop striking them, so you listen to others discover this instead.

sections.heading: The Boulders and the North Bank
paragraphs: The granite here is ancient — around 2.5 billion years old. Wind and water erosion has created formations that look artificial but are not. Some are the size of houses. Some the size of apartment blocks.

Rent a bicycle from the Virupapur Gaddi guesthouses (100 rupees a day) and spend an afternoon just riding through the fields and boulders on the north bank. No itinerary. No must-sees. Just the landscape.
I lost two hours doing exactly this on my second morning and did not notice until I was hungry.

sections.heading: The Ride Back
paragraphs: I took the return route through Hospet and Kurnool differently — heading south toward Bellary first before turning north. This adds distance but takes you through the mining belt and back up through the Deccan plateau in golden hour light.
The kind of ride where you do not want the destination. Best time to visit: October to February. Summer in the ruins is brutal — 40°C with zero shade.

title: Bangalore to Chikmagalur: Coffee, Ghats, and the Ride That Fixes Everything
description: 240 kilometers through the Bababudangiri hills to India's coffee heartland — the perfect reset when city life gets too loud.

sections.heading: Why This Ride Works
paragraphs: There is a specific moment on the Sakleshpur stretch — somewhere around the third tunnel — where the air changes. The exhaust smell disappears, replaced by something green and damp, and the temperature drops four degrees in about thirty seconds. That is when you know you have left Bangalore behind properly.

Chikmagalur sits at the northern tip of the Western Ghats, 240 kilometers from Bangalore. The elevation climbs steadily, the road turns into a series of S-curves through coffee estate country, and by Mudigere the air smells like it should.
This is not a technical ride. It is about 4–5 hours of clean, flowing road through one of the most beautiful agricultural landscapes in South India.

sections.heading: The Route
paragraphs: NH75 via Hassan is faster — smooth highway with a gentle climb. Sakleshpur diversion through the estate roads adds 40 minutes and removes zero enjoyment. Take the Sakleshpur route every time.
The Abbe Falls road near Sakleshpur is worth a separate 20-minute detour. If you go at 7 AM you have the falls to yourself.

sections.heading: Coffee Country
paragraphs: Chikmagalur district grows roughly 70% of Karnataka's coffee output. The estates here are working farms, some running for three or four generations. The Arabica is shade-grown under jackfruit, silver oak, and pepper vines.

I buy from the same small collective near the base of Mullayanagiri every time — half a kilo of Arabica, unwashed process, light roast. It lasts three weeks and makes Monday mornings look forward to themselves.

sections.heading: Mullayanagiri
paragraphs: Karnataka's highest peak at 1,930 meters. The road goes most of the way up — past the treeline into a landscape that looks nothing like the rest of the state. Rocky, windswept, completely silent except for the wind.
Sunrise from the summit is worth the 4:30 AM alarm. You see the Western Ghats spine stretching south and the Deccan plateau fading into haze to the north. Take a jacket — it gets cold fast.

sections.heading: The Best Part
paragraphs: Sitting on the steps of the homestay at 6 PM with a cup of estate coffee, watching the mist come up from the valley below. Not photographing it. Just watching it.
The data pipelines were fine while I was gone. They always are. The trick is believing that temporarily enough to actually sit still. Chikmagalur is good at that.
